title: Hybrid Quantum-Classical Machine Learning Algorithms for Multi-Output Time-Series Forecasting at Utility Scale
authors: Mackenson Polché, Varun Puram, Aditi Lal, Weronika Golletz, Joan Étude Arrow, Vardaan Sahgal, Kumar Ghosh, Giorgio Cortiana, Corey O'Meara
abstract: Multi-output time-series forecasting in energy systems is challenging because of nonlinear dynamics, multi-scale seasonality, and strong dependencies across correlated series. In this work, we investigate two hybrid quantum-classical frameworks for multi-stream time-series forecasting on a real Smart Meter dataset comprising 103 household electricity consumption time-series, with experiments executed on the $ibm\_marrakesh$ superconducting quantum processor. The first model, Kernelized Quantum Reservoir Computing with Repeated Measurement (KQRC-RM), combines coupled quantum reservoirs, ancilla-assisted repeated measurement, and kernelized readouts to model temporal dynamics and cross-stream correlations jointly. For a 3-stream time-series input and output, the KQRC-RM model using 114 qubits achieves an MAE of 0.0811 on MPS simulator (36.92\% improvement over its classical analog) whereas performance degrades to an MAE of 0.1524 on hardware. The second, a Projected Quantum Kernel Gaussian Process (QGP), replaces fidelity-based kernels with projected kernels constructed from local reduced-state statistics. Using a topology-aware 100-qubit QGP model to predict 100 multi-output time-series values, we observe 49\% of time-series outputs achieve high-accuracy predictions (MAE $<0.15$), with an average MAE of $0.082$ for this low-error group. The medium-error regime (MAE $0.15$-$0.35$) has an average MAE of $0.229$, while the high-error regime (MAE $>0.35$) has an average MAE of $0.664$. Overall, this reduces the average MAE relative to the classical GP baseline by 62.01\% on MPS simulator and 40.37\% on hardware. Together, these results demonstrate the feasibility of hybrid quantum machine learning for multi-input, multi-output time-series forecasting at the 100+ qubit scale on NISQ devices.
summary: This paper demonstrates the application of hybrid quantum machine learning to large-scale energy demand forecasting. It shows that while quantum kernels can significantly outperform classical ones in simulation, real-world hardware noise currently limits the practical advantage on NISQ devices.
main_problem: Investigating the feasibility and performance of hybrid quantum-classical machine learning algorithms for multi-output time-series forecasting of energy consumption at a utility scale.
main_result: The QGP model achieved a 62.01% MAE reduction on simulator and 40.37% on hardware compared to classical GP, while KQRC-RM showed significant improvement on simulator but performance degradation on hardware due to device noise.
method: Two hybrid frameworks were tested: Kernelized Quantum Reservoir Computing with Repeated Measurement (KQRC-RM) and Projected Quantum Kernel Gaussian Process (QGP) using local reduced-state statistics.
model_or_system: The experiments were performed on the IBM marrakesh superconducting quantum processor and an MPS simulator, using a Smart Meter dataset of 103 residential electricity consumption time-series.
key_observables: Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Mean Squared Error (MSE), geometric difference (gCQ), and model complexity (kappa).
important_parameters: 100+ qubit scales (114 qubits for KQRC-RM, 100 qubits for QGP), number of system qubits per customer, and hardware noise parameters (T2 dephasing and CZ gate error).
main_assumptions: The QGP model assumes conditional independence across outputs; hardware performance is limited by NISQ-era noise, decoherence, and readout errors.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the KQRC-RM circuit; Figure 2 shows the QGP feature map; Figure 3 displays geometric difference and model complexity; Figure 4 compares simulator vs hardware trajectories; Figure 5 shows per-horizon MAE; Figure 6 shows MSE for 5-customer groups; Figure 7 benchmarks against classical baselines; Figure 8 maps hardware noise; Figure 9 shows energy profiles and customer grouping; Figure 10 shows error vs qubit scaling; Figure 11 shows training loss and forecasting results; Figures 12-13 map the hardware noise landscape.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the forecasting problem, describes the two hybrid quantum architectures (KQRC-RM and QGP), details the experimental setup on IBM hardware and simulators, presents performance benchmarks against classical models, analyzes the impact of hardware noise, and concludes with scalability and utility-scale findings.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Circuit-level view of the proposed Kernelized Quantum Reservoir with repeated measurement. Each input stream 𝑥(𝑠) 𝑡 is encoded into its system register (𝑞𝑠,0, . . . , 𝑞𝑠,𝑛𝑞−1) via a parametrized encoding unitary 𝑈(𝑠) enc. Intra-stream entangling gates propagate information within each register, while inter-stream couplings between boundary qubits introduce cross-stream interactions. Each system qubit is then coupled to a corresponding ancilla qubit (𝑎𝑠,0, . . . , 𝑎𝑠,𝑛𝑞−1), and only the ancillas are measured at each timestep. Repeated measurements
